description: Create the Python ML training pipeline for NAS100 that processes historical OHLCV data, engineers IPDA-based features (order blocks, FVGs, liquidity sweeps, market structure shifts, killzones), trains a lightweight neural network model, and exports weights to a CSV file that the MQL5 EA can load at runtime.
steps: Create Python/nas100_ml_trainer.py in the repository root with the following components:
1. Data loading module: Accept NAS100 M1 OHLCV CSV data (columns: datetime,open,high,low,close,volume). Support chunked reading for large files like analyze_ticks.py does.

2. Feature engineering module implementing IPDA concepts: (a) Market Structure detection - identify swing highs/lows, BOS (break of structure), CHoCH (change of character) on M5/M15/H1 timeframes resampled from M1 data. (b) Order Block detection - last opposing candle before displacement moves. (c) Fair Value Gap detection - 3-candle imbalance patterns. (d) Liquidity sweep detection - price takes out previous highs/lows then reverses. (e) Session/Killzone features - London (08:00-11:00), NY (13:30-16:00) sessions for NAS100. (f) Premium/Discount zones - price position relative to dealing range. (g) Technical indicators: EMA(8,21,50,200), RSI(14), ATR(14), VWAP proxy, volume profile.

3. Label generation: For each M1 bar, create labels based on future price movement over next 5-15 bars. Labels: BUY (+1) if price rises more than 1.5x ATR, SELL (-1) if price falls more than 1.5x ATR, NEUTRAL (0) otherwise.

4. Neural network model: Implement a simple feedforward neural network (3 hidden layers: 128->64->32 neurons with ReLU activation) using only numpy (no tensorflow/pytorch dependency). Include forward pass, backpropagation, and training loop with batch gradient descent.

5. Training pipeline: Split data 80/20 train/test, normalize features with min-max scaling, train for configurable epochs with early stopping, output training metrics (accuracy, precision, recall for each class).

6. Model export: Save trained weights and biases as CSV files in a format the MQL5 EA can read. Export normalization parameters (min/max per feature) so the EA can normalize live data identically. Save to 'NAS100_ML_Model/' directory with files: weights_layer1.csv, weights_layer2.csv, weights_layer3.csv, weights_output.csv, biases_layer1.csv, biases_layer2.csv, biases_layer3.csv, biases_output.csv, normalization_params.csv, feature_names.csv.

7. Configuration at top of script: CSV_FILE path, model hyperparameters (learning_rate, epochs, batch_size, hidden_sizes), IPDA parameters (swing lookback, OB displacement threshold, FVG minimum size).
8. Add comprehensive docstrings and a usage section at the top explaining how to prepare data and run training.
acceptance_criteria: Python script passes ast.parse() validation without errors
Script has all required imports (pandas, numpy, os, sys, json, datetime)
Feature engineering covers all IPDA concepts listed (market structure, OBs, FVGs, liquidity sweeps, killzones, premium/discount)
Neural network implementation includes forward pass and backpropagation using only numpy
Model export format produces CSV files readable by MQL5 FileReadString/FileReadNumber functions
Normalization parameters are exported alongside weights
Script includes clear documentation on data format requirements and usage
verification: Run: python3 -c "import importlib.util; spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location('trainer', '/projects/sandbox/Claude/Python/nas100_ml_trainer.py'); print('Module loadable: PASS')"
findings: Script produces 50 features total covering all IPDA concepts. Pipeline tested end-to-end with synthetic data. pandas and numpy must be installed (pip3 install pandas numpy). Architecture is input(50)->128->64->32->3. The feature_names.csv output documents the exact feature order needed by the MQL5 EA.
